1.what: Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama landed at Calicut (Kozhikode) in 1498, completing the first direct sea route from Europe to India around the Cape of Good Hope.
1.upsc: Marks the beginning of European commercial interest in India. UPSC frequently tests this as the start of the colonial era and the end of Arab monopoly on spice trade.
1.remember: Da Gama was welcomed by the Zamorin of Calicut but faced hostility from Arab traders who saw him as a threat to their monopoly.

54.what: Portuguese admiral Afonso de Albuquerque captured Goa from the Bijapur Sultanate in 1510, making it the first European territorial possession in India.
54.upsc: UPSC tests this as the foundation of Portuguese power in India. Goa remained a Portuguese colony until 1961. a 450-year occupation.
54.remember: Albuquerque encouraged his soldiers to marry Indian women. a deliberate policy to create a loyal mixed population.

55.what: Queen Elizabeth I granted a royal charter to the East India Company on 31 December 1600, giving it a monopoly on English trade with Asia.
55.upsc: The EIC's transformation from a trading company to a ruling power is central to UPSC. Know the key dates: 1600 (founded), 1757 (Plassey), 1857 (end of Company rule).
55.remember: It started as a trading company with 125 shareholders. It ended up ruling 250 million people.

56.what: The Battle of Buxar in 1764 saw the EIC defeat the combined forces of the Nawab of Oudh, the Mughal Emperor, and the Nawab of Bengal. a far more significant victory than Plassey.
56.upsc: More important than Plassey for UPSC purposes. The Treaty of Allahabad (1765) that followed gave the EIC Diwani rights over Bengal, Bihar and Orissa. the right to collect revenue.
56.remember: Plassey gave the EIC political dominance. Buxar gave it financial control. Buxar was the real turning point.

57.what: The Regulating Act of 1773 was the first attempt by the British Parliament to control the EIC. It created the post of Governor-General of Bengal (Warren Hastings was the first).
57.upsc: First constitutional recognition of EIC's political role. UPSC tests the Supreme Court created at Calcutta and the relationship between the Governor-General and Council.
57.remember: The Act failed because the Governor-General could be outvoted by his own council. a design flaw fixed by Pitt's India Act in 1784.

58.what: Pitt's India Act of 1784 created a Board of Control in London with real authority over the EIC's political (not commercial) affairs, establishing dual government.
58.upsc: Key Act for UPSC. it distinguished between commercial and political functions for the first time. The Board of Control represented Parliament's supremacy over the Company.
58.remember: Pitt's Act worked where the Regulating Act failed. The Governor-General now had overriding powers over his Council.

59.what: Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement in 1793, fixing land revenue from Bengal permanently. Zamindars became hereditary owners of land provided they paid a fixed tax.
59.upsc: Created a new class of loyal zamindars but impoverished peasants and stifled agricultural investment. UPSC tests its impact on rural Bengal and why it failed in other regions.
59.remember: Revenue was fixed forever: great for zamindars in good years, ruinous when harvests failed. Peasants lost all customary rights.

60.what: Lord Wellesley introduced the Subsidiary Alliance system where Indian rulers accepted British troops for protection, paid for them, gave up independent foreign policy, and accepted a British Resident.
60.upsc: One of the most tested expansion tools. Hyderabad was the first to sign (1798). It made Indian states financially dependent and politically subordinate without formal annexation.
60.remember: The alliance was a debt trap: rulers could rarely afford the troops, fell into arrears, and had to cede territory to pay.

61.what: The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War ended when Tipu Sultan was killed defending his capital Seringapatam in 1799. Mysore was partitioned: half to the EIC, half restored to the Hindu Wodeyar dynasty.
61.upsc: Tipu Sultan is a recurring UPSC personality: his rockets, his resistance, his death. Know all four Anglo-Mysore Wars in sequence.
61.remember: Tipu died fighting at the gates of his own fort. He told his commanders: 'Better to live one day as a tiger than a thousand years as a sheep.'

62.what: The Second Anglo-Maratha War (1803-1805) broke up the Maratha confederacy. The British defeated Sindhia and Bhonsle, gaining control of Delhi and much of central India.
62.upsc: The three Anglo-Maratha Wars (1775, 1803, 1817) are a UPSC staple. The Second War gave the British the old Mughal capital Delhi and the area around Agra.
62.remember: After this war, the blind Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II became a British pensioner living under Company protection in Delhi.

63.what: The Charter Act of 1813 ended the EIC's trade monopoly in India (except for tea and China trade). It also allowed Christian missionaries to enter India and allocated funds for Indian education.
63.upsc: Multiple UPSC angles: end of monopoly, education clause, missionary entry. The 1813 Act is often paired with the 1833 Act (which ended all trading activities of EIC).
63.remember: The Act set aside one lakh rupees annually for Indian education. the seed of the great education debate between Orientalists and Anglicists.

64.what: Lord Dalhousie's Doctrine of Lapse held that if a ruler died without a natural heir, the state would lapse to the EIC. It was used to annex Satara, Jhansi, Nagpur and others.
64.upsc: Direct cause of 1857 revolt. Rani Laxmibai of Jhansi lost her kingdom this way. UPSC loves asking which states were annexed under Doctrine of Lapse.
64.remember: Dalhousie also annexed Oudh (Awadh) in 1856: not under Doctrine of Lapse but for 'misgovernance'. This alienated the sepoys whose families were from Oudh.

65.what: The Revolt of 1857 began on 10 May at Meerut when sepoys refused to use greased cartridges and marched to Delhi, where they proclaimed Bahadur Shah Zafar as Emperor of Hindustan.
65.upsc: The most-tested event in Colonial India. Know the causes (cartridges, Doctrine of Lapse, Oudh), leaders (Rani Laxmibai, Tantia Tope, Nana Sahib), and why it failed.
65.remember: The cartridges were reportedly greased with cow and pig fat: offensive to Hindu and Muslim sepoys alike. Whether true or not, the rumour was enough.

66.what: After the 1857 revolt, the EIC was dissolved and India came under direct British Crown rule. Queen Victoria's Proclamation promised non-interference in religion and equal treatment under law.
66.upsc: Marks the beginning of the British Raj. The Secretary of State for India replaced the Board of Control. The Governor-General became Viceroy.
66.remember: Bahadur Shah Zafar, the last Mughal, was exiled to Rangoon (Burma) where he died in 1862. The 300-year Mughal dynasty ended in a Burmese jail.

67.what: The Indian Councils Act of 1861 introduced legislative councils at the centre and provinces, and allowed Indians to be nominated (not elected) to them for the first time.
67.upsc: First formal inclusion of Indians in legislation: though purely advisory. UPSC tests the sequence: 1861, 1892, 1909, 1919, 1935 Acts as steps toward self-government.
67.remember: The Act also restored the portfolio system in the Viceroy's Executive Council: individual members took charge of specific departments.

68.what: The Indian National Congress was founded in 1885 by A.O. Hume, a retired ICS officer, with 72 delegates meeting in Bombay. Womesh Bonerjee was the first president.
68.upsc: Foundation of India's independence movement. Early Congress was moderate: petitions, not protests. UPSC tests the split between Moderates and Extremists (1907, Surat).
68.remember: Hume founded Congress partly to give educated Indians a 'safety valve' for grievances. The British later regretted enabling it.

69.what: The Age of Consent Act of 1891 raised the age of consent for girls from 10 to 12. It was a response to child marriage and sparked fierce debate between reformers and orthodox Hindus.
69.upsc: Tests the social reform vs. religious orthodoxy tension in colonial India. Bal Gangadhar Tilak opposed it as interference in Hindu custom.
69.remember: The act followed the Phulmoni case. a 10-year-old girl died from injuries inflicted by her 35-year-old husband. The public outrage forced legislative action.

70.what: Lord Curzon partitioned Bengal in 1905 into East Bengal (Muslim majority) and West Bengal (Hindu majority), officially for administrative efficiency but widely seen as divide and rule.
70.upsc: Immediate cause of the Swadeshi Movement. UPSC tests Curzon's justification vs. Indian perception, and the annulment in 1911.
70.remember: Curzon called Bengal 'too large to administer.' Indians called it a deliberate attempt to split Hindu-Muslim unity in the most politically active province.

71.what: The Swadeshi Movement of 1905 called for boycott of British goods and promotion of Indian-made products. It was the first mass political movement in India.
71.upsc: Marks the shift from moderate petitioning to mass action. UPSC tests the economic rationale, the role of Tilak, Bipin Pal and Lajpat Rai (Lal-Bal-Pal).
71.remember: Bonfire of British cloth was a defining image. The movement pioneered methods: boycott, passive resistance. that Gandhi would later use nationally.

72.what: The All India Muslim League was founded at Dhaka in 1906 by Nawab Salimullah Khan. Its initial goal was protecting Muslim political interests under British rule.
72.upsc: UPSC traces its evolution: 1906 (loyalty to British) to 1940 (Pakistan Resolution) to 1947 (Pakistan). Understand the turning points and key leaders.
72.remember: The League was founded at a gathering convened specifically to counter Congress's claim to represent all Indians. Aga Khan III was its first president.

73.what: The Morley-Minto Reforms (Indian Councils Act 1909) introduced separate Muslim electorates for the first time: Muslims would vote for reserved Muslim seats.
73.upsc: The most consequential reform before 1919. Separate electorates institutionalised communal voting and are blamed for deepening Hindu-Muslim division.
73.remember: Morley (Secretary of State) said he did not want parliamentary government for India. Yet his reforms set India on exactly that path.

74.what: King George V announced the shifting of India's capital from Calcutta to Delhi during the Delhi Durbar of 1911. The Partition of Bengal was also annulled at the same event.
74.upsc: Tests two events at once: capital shift and Bengal reunification. UPSC often asks why Delhi was chosen: its historical symbolism as the Mughal capital.
74.remember: Edwin Lutyens was commissioned to design New Delhi. It took 20 years to build and was inaugurated in 1931: ironically just as the independence movement peaked.

75.what: The Lucknow Pact of 1916 was an agreement between Congress and the Muslim League. Congress accepted separate electorates; the League joined the nationalist mainstream.
75.upsc: Rare Congress-League unity. Tilak (Extremist) and Jinnah (League) were its architects: ironic given later Hindu-Muslim conflict. Tests Congress's concessions.
75.remember: Jinnah was called 'Ambassador of Hindu-Muslim Unity' at this time. The same man would demand Pakistan 24 years later.

76.what: The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (Government of India Act 1919) introduced dyarchy: provincial subjects split between transferred (Indian ministers) and reserved (British control) categories.
76.upsc: Dyarchy is heavily tested: which subjects were transferred vs reserved. Introduced bicameral legislature at centre. Direct elections for first time.
76.remember: Dyarchy failed because the important subjects (law, finance, order) were 'reserved' for British officials. Indian ministers got agriculture and education: less powerful portfolios.

77.what: The Rowlatt Act of 1919 allowed the British to imprison any person without trial for two years, as a wartime emergency measure extended into peacetime.
77.upsc: Triggered Gandhi's first all-India agitation. UPSC tests the 'Black Act' tag, the hartal called, and why Gandhi suspended it after Chauri Chaura (1922).
77.remember: Indians called it the 'No appeal, no vakeel, no daleel' act: no appeal, no lawyer, no argument. Gandhi called a hartal on 6 April 1919. Jallianwala Bagh followed three days later.

78.what: On 13 April 1919, General Dyer ordered troops to fire without warning on a peaceful crowd gathered at Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar. Official count: 379 dead. Actual estimates: over 1,000.
78.upsc: One of the most tested events. Know: date (13 April: Baisakhi), location, Dyer's justification, Hunter Commission findings, Udham Singh's revenge (1940).
78.remember: Dyer blocked the only exit before ordering firing. He faced no criminal charges in Britain. the House of Lords praised him. Rabindranath Tagore returned his knighthood in protest.

79.what: The Khilafat Movement (1919-1922) was led by Mohammad Ali and Shaukat Ali to protect the Ottoman Caliphate after WWI. Gandhi supported it to forge Hindu-Muslim unity.
79.upsc: Tests Gandhi's strategy and Hindu-Muslim relations. The movement collapsed when Turkey's Mustafa Kemal himself abolished the Caliphate in 1924.
79.remember: The irony: Indians fought to protect the Turkish Sultan-Caliph, but it was the Turks: not the British: who abolished the Caliphate three years later.

80.what: Gandhi launched the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1920 calling for boycott of British institutions: courts, schools, councils, goods. It was the first mass civil disobedience movement.
80.upsc: Tests Gandhi's methods, the scale of participation, and why it was suspended. Know the four-stage plan Gandhi outlined and how it was disrupted by Chauri Chaura.
80.remember: Lawyers boycotted courts, students left British schools, people surrendered titles. For the first time, the British felt genuinely threatened.

81.what: At Chauri Chaura in UP, a police firing on protesters triggered a mob that burned a police station alive with 22 policemen inside. Gandhi immediately suspended the Non-Cooperation Movement.
81.upsc: Tests Gandhi's commitment to non-violence above political gains. His decision to suspend the movement was controversial: many leaders including Nehru were in jail and disagreed.
81.remember: Gandhi called it a 'Himalayan blunder': not Chauri Chaura itself, but launching the movement before Indians were truly ready for non-violent discipline.

82.what: The Simon Commission arrived in 1928 to review the 1919 Act. It had no Indian members. a deliberate exclusion that unified Congress and League in protest.
82.upsc: Tests the all-white composition as cause of boycott, the 'Simon Go Back' slogan, and Lala Lajpat Rai's death after a police lathi charge at Lahore.
82.remember: Lajpat Rai was beaten by police during a protest. He died weeks later and reportedly said: 'Every blow struck on my body will be the last nail in the coffin of British imperialism.'

83.what: At the Lahore Session of December 1929, Congress under Nehru declared Purna Swaraj (complete independence) as its goal. The tricolour was unfurled on 26 January 1930.
83.upsc: 26 January 1930 was the first Independence Day celebration. This is why 26 January was chosen as Republic Day in 1950. UPSC connects these dates.
83.remember: Nehru hoisted the tricolour on the banks of the Ravi river at midnight on 31 December 1929. Independence Day was celebrated on 26 January every year until 1947.

84.what: Gandhi's Dandi March began on 12 March 1930. a 241-mile walk from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi beach to make salt from seawater, defying the British salt tax.
84.upsc: Most symbolically powerful act of civil disobedience. Tests the 11 demands Gandhi sent to Viceroy Irwin, the mass arrests, and international media coverage.
84.remember: Salt was chosen brilliantly: everyone uses it, it was taxed unfairly, and making it was illegal. It united the poor and the rich in one simple act of defiance.

85.what: The First Round Table Conference met in London in November 1930 to discuss India's constitutional future. Congress boycotted it: Gandhi was in jail.
85.upsc: Three Round Table Conferences (1930, 1931, 1932). Only the second had Congress representation (Gandhi attended). All three failed to produce a agreed constitution.
85.remember: The British invited everyone except Congress: and then found that without Congress, no agreement was possible. Gandhi was released and the Gandhi-Irwin Pact followed.

86.what: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact of March 1931 suspended the Civil Disobedience Movement. Gandhi agreed to attend the Second Round Table Conference; the British released political prisoners.
86.upsc: Tests the terms and failures. Bhagat Singh's execution (23 March 1931) was not stayed despite Gandhi's request. a major controversy and source of criticism.
86.remember: The Pact was seen as a climbdown by many: especially since Bhagat Singh was hanged just days after it was signed. Subhash Bose and others called it a betrayal.

87.what: The Poona Pact of 1932 was signed between Gandhi and Dr. Ambedkar after Gandhi's fast-unto-death protesting the British granting separate electorates for Depressed Classes.
87.upsc: Tests the conflict between Gandhi and Ambedkar on untouchability and representation. Ambedkar gave up separate electorates but secured more reserved seats in joint electorates.
87.remember: Ambedkar resented the Pact his whole life: he felt he was emotionally coerced. He said Gandhi used his own life as a political weapon to deny Dalits self-determination.

88.what: The Government of India Act 1935 was the longest Act of British Parliament. It abolished dyarchy at provinces, introduced federation (never implemented), and gave provinces significant autonomy.
88.upsc: Blueprint for India's constitution. Most provisions of the 1950 Constitution came from this Act. Know what was new: Federal Court, RBI, provincial autonomy.
88.remember: The Act had 321 sections and 10 schedules: longer than the Indian Constitution itself. Congress accepted provincial autonomy but rejected the federal provisions.

89.what: Congress won majorities in 7 of 11 provinces in the 1937 elections under the 1935 Act, forming governments across most of British India for the first time.
89.upsc: Tests the election results, Congress's two years in power (1937-1939), and why Congress ministries resigned in 1939 (protesting India being dragged into WWII without consultation).
89.remember: Jinnah demanded Congress form coalition governments with the League. Congress refused. This refusal, combined with Congress ministries' alleged pro-Hindu policies, fuelled League's Pakistan demand.

90.what: Sir Stafford Cripps came to India in 1942 with an offer of Dominion Status after the war in exchange for Indian cooperation in WWII. Congress and League both rejected it.
90.upsc: Tests why it was rejected: Congress called it a 'post-dated cheque on a failing bank' (Gandhi). The right to secede was offered to provinces: unacceptable to Congress.
90.remember: Congress wanted immediate independence. The League wanted Pakistan guaranteed. Cripps satisfied neither. The failure directly led to the Quit India Movement five months later.

91.what: Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement on 8 August 1942 with the call 'Do or Die.' All top Congress leaders were arrested within hours. Underground resistance spread across India.
91.upsc: Most spontaneous uprising since 1857. Tests 'Do or Die', August Kranti, Aruna Asaf Ali (hoisted flag at Gowalia Tank), and underground leaders like Jayaprakash Narayan.
91.remember: Gandhi was arrested at 5 AM on 9 August before he could give his planned speech. The movement continued without leadership for 3 years.

92.what: INA (Indian National Army) officers were tried for treason at the Red Fort in Delhi in 1945-46. The trials backfired: making the accused national heroes.
92.upsc: The trials triggered the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny (1946) and showed the British that Indian armed forces could no longer be relied upon. Accelerated independence timeline.
92.remember: Nehru, Bhulabhai Desai and others defended the INA officers as lawyers. The public response was overwhelmingly pro-INA. Three officers: Shah Nawaz, PK Sehgal, GS Dhillon: became famous.

93.what: The Cabinet Mission Plan of 1946 proposed a three-tier federal structure: provinces, groups of provinces, and centre: to keep India united while addressing Muslim concerns.
93.upsc: Complex plan worth memorising in detail. Congress accepted in principle but rejected compulsory grouping. League first accepted then rejected. Failure led to Direct Action Day.
93.remember: This was Britain's last serious attempt to keep India united. Its failure made partition virtually inevitable.

94.what: Jinnah called 16 August 1946 as Direct Action Day to press the Pakistan demand. In Calcutta, communal riots broke out: 'The Great Calcutta Killings' killed 5,000-10,000 people in 4 days.
94.upsc: Marks the point of no return toward partition. Tests Jinnah's calculation, the Bengal government's (Muslim League in power) alleged inaction, and the chain reaction in other regions.
94.remember: The violence spread to Noakhali, Bihar and Garhmukteshwar. Gandhi walked village to village in Noakhali to stop the killing. Partition had begun before independence.

95.what: The Mountbatten Plan of 3 June 1947 announced the partition of India into India and Pakistan. Boundary demarcation was given to Cyril Radcliffe who had never visited India.
95.upsc: Tests Mountbatten's role, why Congress accepted partition, the 3 June plan details (referendums for NWFP and Sylhet), and the Radcliffe Line.
95.remember: Radcliffe had six weeks to divide a subcontinent of 400 million people. He had never been to India before. The boundaries were announced two days after independence.

96.what: India became independent on 15 August 1947 at midnight. Nehru delivered his 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at Parliament. The Union Jack was lowered across India.
96.upsc: Know: midnight session (12 AM 15 August), Nehru's speech, Mountbatten as first Governor-General of India, Rajendra Prasad as President of Constituent Assembly.
96.remember: The date was chosen by Mountbatten. Pakistan became independent on 14 August. Both share the same Independence Day origin: midnight straddles both dates.

97.what: Subhas Chandra Bose formed the Indian National Army (INA/Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943 in Southeast Asia from Indian POWs captured by Japan, with the goal of liberating India from the east.
97.upsc: Tests INA's formation, Bose's 'Give me blood, I will give you freedom' speech, the Azad Hind government, and his disputed death in 1945.
97.remember: Bose's INA was diverse: men and women, Hindus, Muslims and Sikhs, fighting together. The Rani of Jhansi Regiment was an all-women combat unit. the first in Asia.
